children.title: Document Root
children.summary: The Import Tax Expenditure Report, prepared by the Tax Policy Directorate of Ethiopia's Ministry of Finance in collaboration with the IFS Centre for Tax Analysis in Developing Countries, presents tax expenditure estimates for FY 2018/19 to 2020/21, covering customs duty, excise duty, VAT, and sur.

metadata.summary: The Import Tax Expenditure Report, prepared by the Tax Policy Directorate of Ethiopia's Ministry of Finance in collaboration with the IFS Centre for Tax Analysis in Developing Countries, presents tax expenditure estimates for FY 2018/19 to 2020/21, covering customs duty, excise duty, VAT, and sur.

children.title: Executive summary ...................................................................................................................... 4
children.summary: The executive summary section introduces a report estimating import tax expenditures for fiscal years 2018/19 to 2020/21, covering customs duty, excise duty, VAT, and surtax, and provides an overview of the report's methodology and scope.

metadata.summary: The executive summary section introduces a report estimating import tax expenditures for fiscal years 2018/19 to 2020/21, covering customs duty, excise duty, VAT, and surtax, and provides an overview of the report's methodology and scope.

children.title: Executive summary
children.summary: The Ethiopian government incurred significant import tax expenditures, totaling ETB 120.7 billion in FY 2020/21, with the largest shares attributed to VAT, customs duty, excise tax, and surtax expenditures, which are estimated to be around 2.8% of GDP and are primarily driven by ad.

metadata.summary: The Ethiopian government incurred significant import tax expenditures, totaling ETB 120.7 billion in FY 2020/21, with the largest shares attributed to VAT, customs duty, excise tax, and surtax expenditures, which are estimated to be around 2.8% of GDP and are primarily driven by ad.

children.title: For details, see Appendix D.
children.summary: The Ethiopian Ministry of Finance's tax expenditure report for 2018/19 to 2020/21 details the country's tax policies, including exemptions and incentives, aimed at promoting investment, particularly in the manufacturing sector, and improving international competitiveness, with the goal of transforming the Ethiopian economy and improving living standards.

metadata.summary: The Ethiopian Ministry of Finance's tax expenditure report for 2018/19 to 2020/21 details the country's tax policies, including exemptions and incentives, aimed at promoting investment, particularly in the manufacturing sector, and improving international competitiveness, with the goal of transforming the Ethiopian economy and improving living standards.

children.summary: The section discusses definitions and data related to tax expenditures, including the revenue forgone method used to estimate them, and the definition of a benchmark tax system, which involves judgement calls that affect the resulting estimates, particularly in the context of import taxes and customs duty.

children.summary: The section discusses Ethiopia's import tax system, including customs duty, excise tax, and VAT, which are governed by various proclamations and trade agreements, such as the Common Market for Eastern and Southern Africa (COMESA) Preferential Trade Agreement, and are applied sequentially to affect the tax base and revenue collection.

metadata.summary: The section discusses Ethiopia's import tax system, including customs duty, excise tax, and VAT, which are governed by various proclamations and trade agreements, such as the Common Market for Eastern and Southern Africa (COMESA) Preferential Trade Agreement, and are applied sequentially to affect the tax base and revenue collection.

children.title: are reported in Appendix C.)
children.summary: The section discusses customs duty tax expenditures in Ethiopia, calculated using a formula that considers the CIF value, customs duty, excise tax, and VAT payable, with certain imports exempt from surtax, such as fertilizers and petroleum, as per the Council of Ministers Regulation No. 133/2007.

metadata.summary: The section discusses customs duty tax expenditures in Ethiopia, calculated using a formula that considers the CIF value, customs duty, excise tax, and VAT payable, with certain imports exempt from surtax, such as fertilizers and petroleum, as per the Council of Ministers Regulation No. 133/2007.

children.title: Appendix B.
children.summary: This section, Appendix B, discusses customs duty tax expenditures and excise tax expenditures, explaining how they are computed based on benchmark duty rates, actual rates charged, and tax bases, using variables such as τ, CIF, and IE to represent customs duty rates, cost, insurance, and freight, and import expenses.

children.summary: The report utilizes shipment-level microdata from the Ethiopian Customs Commission, covering FY 2018/19 to FY 2020/21, which was accessed from the Automated System for Customs Data and the electronic Customs Management System, containing details such as importer information and tax expenditures.

children.title: Note: Assumes no duplicate reporting of transactions across customs IT systems (see Appendix D).
children.summary: The Ethiopian Customs Commission reported a 54% increase in tax expenditures in FY 2020/21, with customs duty, excise, VAT, and surtax expenditures totaling ETB 78 billion in FY 2019/20 and increasing to ETB 120.7 billion in FY 2020/21.

metadata.summary: The Ethiopian Customs Commission reported a 54% increase in tax expenditures in FY 2020/21, with customs duty, excise, VAT, and surtax expenditures totaling ETB 78 billion in FY 2019/20 and increasing to ETB 120.7 billion in FY 2020/21.

children.summary: The section discusses tax expenditures as a share of GDP and imports from FY 2018/19 to FY 2020/21, categorizing imports into capital/investment, second schedule, and non-capital/second schedule based on the Customs Procedure Code (CPC), while noting evidence of substantial duplicate recording of imports.

children.title: expenditures across categories are given in Table F.5 in Appendix F.
children.summary: The Ministry of Finance, Ethiopia, reports total expenditures across various categories, including imports, capital investments, and specific commodity groups such as food, mineral oil, and machinery, as detailed in Table F.5 in Appendix F.

children.title: transactions across customs IT systems (see Appendix D).
children.summary: The section discusses transactions across customs IT systems, specifically analyzing tax expenditures in Ethiopia from FY 2018/19 to FY 2020/21, with notable year-on-year variations in categories such as vehicle imports, animal or vegetable fats, and non-electrical machinery, influenced by government policy and changes in import values.

children.title: Appendix E. Assumes no duplicate reporting of transactions across customs IT systems (see Appendix D).
children.summary: The Ethiopian Customs Commission's data reveals that tax expenditures as a share of import value varied significantly across commodity groups in FY 2020/21, with the highest shares observed for vehicles at 52.3% and animal or vegetable fats at 48.1%.

metadata.summary: The Ethiopian Customs Commission's data reveals that tax expenditures as a share of import value varied significantly across commodity groups in FY 2020/21, with the highest shares observed for vehicles at 52.3% and animal or vegetable fats at 48.1%.
